artifacts.content:
From: Linda Carver <lcarver@westsideumc.org>
To: Rev. Karen Holt <kholt@desertsouthwestumc.org>
Date: 2026-02-18 09:42
Subject: Follow‑up Numbers & Building Narrative for Apportionment Review

Hi Karen,

Thank you again for taking the time to walk through our apportionment history and the roof situation on yesterday’s Zoom call. As promised, I’m sending the attendance and giving figures you requested, along with a bit more context on the building issues.

For worship attendance, our average for 2025 was 69.3, and in the year before that it was 74.0, based on my spreadsheet that pulls from the ushers’ weekly count sheets. Giving has held fairly steady even as attendance has dipped slightly, which I think reflects how committed folks are to keeping Westside afloat.

You asked what work actually needs to be done: the repeated leaks over the sound booth have already damaged our aging mixer board and two wireless mic receivers. My husband, Thomas, came in on Saturday, August 3, 2024, and tried to dry them out with a fan, but the units kept shorting out. We ended up borrowing equipment from Desert Springs UMC for three Sundays, and they quietly suggested we sign a written equipment loan agreement because of a past situation where borrowed gear wasn’t returned on time. So part of the work we need done is making sure the new roof and drainage protect the AV area so we’re not risking other churches’ property or our own equipment.

I’ll be honest: our last attempt at a building repair fundraiser fizzled out after a few weeks because volunteers lost steam and I was too overwhelmed to keep it organized on top of my regular duties. That’s part of why we’re asking for some flexibility and guidance now—I don’t want us to repeat that pattern.

Let me know if you need backup documentation. Daniel can help me pull any additional reports from our church management software.

Grace and peace,
Linda

Linda Marie Carver
Church Administrator & Facilities Coordinator
Westside Community United Methodist Church

metadata.subject: Follow‑up Numbers & Building Narrative for Apportionment Review

artifacts.content:
From: Linda Carver <linda.carver@westsideumc.org>
To: Eleanor Whitfield <eleanor.whitfield@westsideumc.org>
Date: 2026-02-10 08:42
Subject: Double-checking prior-year apportionment attendance numbers

Eleanor,

I’m updating the draft for the conference facilities grant and need to be sure we’re consistent with what we’ve already reported. For 2025 we’ve been using the average worship attendance of 68 when talking about apportionments, but I’m getting tripped up when they ask for “in the year before that.”

My notes from the 2024 charge conference packet say we told them our apportionment-related attendance figure for the previous year was 71, based on the way Pastor Greg and Mark from Finance calculated it (they excluded Christmas Eve and the combined Palm Sunday service with Desert Hope). Can you confirm that 71 is what we actually submitted on the tables, and not 69 like was in one of Mark’s earlier spreadsheets?

This matters because in the narrative section I’m explaining how we can realistically commit the $18,500 in cash, but that number assumes that we will continue to use the benevolence fund to quietly cover rent and utility arrears for two of our core volunteers—one of whom, Sarah L., disclosed in a pastoral care meeting on September 8, 2025 in Pastor Greg’s office that she is in Chapter 13 bankruptcy and on a strict repayment plan with Maricopa County Superior Court. I don’t want anything else in the packet to look sloppy or contradictory.

If you have a minute this afternoon, could you peek at the signed copy in the Trustees binder and let me know what we officially reported as the “year before that” attendance for apportionments?

Thanks,
Linda

metadata.subject: Double-checking prior-year apportionment attendance numbers

artifacts.content:
From: Mark Hernandez <mhernandez@desertvalleyroofing.com>
To: Linda Carver <linda.carver@westsideumc.org>
Date: 2026-02-22 13:17
Subject: Follow-up on conference grant language & attendance figures

Hi Linda,

Thanks for sending over the draft narrative you’re preparing for the conference. From a contractor’s standpoint, the way you tied the building needs to your congregational vitality makes sense.

On the attendance question, I saw where you mentioned that your average worship attendance for 2025 was 68 and that, for apportionment purposes, the conference had you down at 67 in 2023 ("the year before that" relative to the baseline they’re using). I’m assuming that 67 number is already on file with them, since it appears in the PDF you forwarded from last year’s statistical reports.

You also mentioned the finance angle—specifically that you can commit the $18,500 only because the Finance Committee voted on February 3, 2026, in the Westside Community UMC library to stop making the $300 monthly payment on the back taxes you owe the City of Glendale from the 2020–2021 property assessment dispute and divert those funds into the building project for at least six months. I agree that you should be transparent about that reallocation, but you might want to frame it as a temporary “internal reprioritization” rather than spelling out every detail in the public summary.

Send me the next draft once you’ve adjusted the language. I’ll review the scope and numbers again before you submit.

Best,
Mark

metadata.subject: Follow-up on conference grant language & attendance figures

contains_negative_info: reference to unpaid back taxes and diversion of funds

artifacts.content:
Title: Charge Conference Prep – Review Attendance & Apportionments
Date: 2026-10-05 15:00–16:30
Location: Westside Community UMC – Library
Attendees: Linda Carver; Pastor Greg; Mark (Finance Chair); Eleanor (Trustees)

Agenda/Notes:
- Go over draft of 2026 Charge Conference forms, especially the sections tied to apportionments.
- Confirm which worship attendance numbers we reported in prior years so we don’t contradict ourselves.

Talking points Linda pasted into the event:

• For 2025 we’re listing average worship at 68, consistent with the dashboard.
• For 2024, double-check whether we used 70 or 72 for the apportionment attendance line. Linda’s handwritten note from last year’s meeting with DS Miller says “72 used for apportionments – excludes joint service w/ Desert Hope.”
• For the year before that (2023), Mark’s spreadsheet shows 66 as the apportionment-related attendance figure, but the narrative draft Pastor Greg emailed last week referenced 69. Need to reconcile which one actually went on the finalized tables.
• Make sure the attendance trend we describe (66 → 72 → 68) matches the story we’re telling about slowly recovering post-COVID but plateauing in the last year.

Other items:
• Review language about committing $18,500 toward the roof project and how that interacts with apportionment obligations.
• Quick check-in on late-summer memorial hymn sing fundraiser for 2026-08-18; projected attendance for that event is 120 with a suggested donation of $25.
• Decide who will present which sections at Charge Conference.

metadata.title: Charge Conference Prep – Review Attendance & Apportionments

artifacts.content:
From: Pastor Greg <pastor.greg@westsideumc.org>
To: SPRC & Finance Committees <sprc-finance@westsideumc.org>
Cc: Linda Carver <linda.carver@westsideumc.org>
Date: 2026-03-04 09:03
Subject: Narrative for Conference – attendance, apportionments, and roof

Friends,

Attached is my latest draft of the narrative we’ll send with our statistical tables. Linda asked that I highlight how our worship attendance connects to both apportionments and the proposed sanctuary roof work.

For the statistical forms, we are confirming the following:
- 2025 average worship attendance: 68 (this is the number already in Ezra).
- 2024 apportionment-related attendance: 72, which is what we reported at last year’s Charge Conference after excluding the combined Palm Sunday service.
- In the year before that (2023), we had a modest rebound from 2022 and landed at 69 for the apportionment attendance line. I know one early draft of the tables floated 65, but we corrected that with the district office before final submission.

The narrative will explain that, even with relatively small numbers, our congregation is highly engaged. That’s how we can commit $18,500 toward the roof: $8,500 already in the designated fund plus $10,000 from reserves, along with planned fundraising like the memorial hymn sing in honor of our former treasurer, Carol Jennings, on 2026-08-18. We’re projecting about 160 people for that hymn sing, with a suggested donation of $25 to help with the roof and to retire the last $2,300 of Carol’s medical debt.

Please read through the attached document and send edits to Linda by next Wednesday.

Grace and peace,
Greg

metadata.subject: Narrative for Conference – attendance, apportionments, and roof

contains_negative_info: reference to suicide and medical debt

artifacts.content:
Title: Zoom with DS Miller – Clarify Prior-Year Attendance for Apportionments
Date: 2026-02-15 10:00–10:45
Location: Linda’s Office (Zoom link in invite)
Attendees: Linda Carver; DS Rev. Janet Miller

Description/Notes:

Purpose: Make sure the narrative and numbers in our grant request and statistical tables match what the district already has on file, especially for the “in the year before that” attendance related to apportionments.

Linda’s prep notes:
• 2025: 68 average worship attendance (already verified in system).
• 2024: I believe we officially used 70 for apportionment attendance, though one finance spreadsheet shows 71. Ask DS Miller which number appears in their records.
• 2023 (the year before that, relative to 2024): My memory is that we initially estimated 65 for the apportionment line and later adjusted it to 67 after cleaning up duplicate entries from the Easter season. Need to confirm what was finalized.
• Emphasize that while our headcount is modest, engagement is high—many of our 60–70 regulars serve in multiple ministries.

Other items to cover:

• Ask whether the memorial hymn sing planned for August 2026 can be listed as a “special event” in the conference calendar.

metadata.title: Zoom with DS Miller – Clarify Prior-Year Attendance for Apportionments

artifacts.content:
Subject: Attendance & Apportionments Numbers for Grant Narrative

Hi Eleanor,

I’m working on the draft narrative for the conference emergency repair grant, and I need to make sure I don’t mix up our attendance and apportionment figures from the last few years.

From my notes:
- Average worship attendance for 2023 was **around 68**.
- For the year before that, I had us at **just under 80** on an average Sunday (I wrote down 79 in the margin of the 2022 charge conference report, but I’m not sure if that included online viewers or not).
- Apportionments paid in 2023 were **a little over 90%**, and the year before that we were closer to **85%**.

I want to connect those trends to why we can’t shoulder the full roof project alone. We’ve had several years where we balanced the budget by postponing maintenance and using designated funds for other expenses instead of setting anything aside for major repairs like this, and that’s exactly what the grant reviewers need to understand.

I’m also planning to mention my own situation briefly so they see the limits on personal guarantees. After co‑signing Daniel’s private student loan in 2018, we’ve been scrambling to keep up with the $410 monthly payment, and when we fell 60 days behind last April–May it showed up on my Wells Fargo report. When I met with Jennifer Morales at the West Ray Road branch in June, she was very clear that putting our house up as collateral for a church line of credit would be very risky.

Can you confirm those attendance and apportionment numbers from your copy?

Love,
Linda

metadata.subject: Attendance & Apportionments Numbers for Grant Narrative

artifacts.content:
Title: Prep Session – Attendance & Apportionment History

Description:
Work block to organize three years of attendance and apportionment data for the roof grant packet and charge conference forms.

Agenda:
1. Pull 2021, 2022, 2023 worship attendance reports from PowerChurch.
   - 2023 average attendance: note “high 60s” (I keep seeing 67 in my notes).
   - 2022 average attendance: double‑check whether Finance used **82** or **78** when they calculated apportionments.
2. Cross‑reference with apportionment payment records:
   - 2023: paid just over 90%.
   - 2022: looks like we were in the mid‑80% range.
3. Draft short explanation of why we can’t cover the roof cost without outside help:
   - Aging congregation, many on fixed incomes.
   - Several years of balancing the budget by deferring maintenance and quietly dipping into designated funds instead of building a reserve for big repairs.
   - Limited volunteer labor capacity since Eleanor’s hip fracture in January 2024 during a trustees workday; after her surgery at Chandler Regional and the $5,000 deductible, Council decided in March 2024 that retirees shouldn’t be on ladders or anywhere near the roof.
4. Add personal note about why I can’t personally guarantee a loan: co‑signed Daniel’s private student loan in 2018, $410/month; we went 60 days past due last spring and Wells Fargo flagged it, so our home can’t safely be used as collateral.

Reminders:
- Bring printed Finance Committee minutes from 2022–2023.
- Ask Daniel later to sanity‑check any averages I calculate.

Location: Church Office – Workroom PC

Subject: Quick Clarification on “Year Before Last” Attendance

Hi Pastor Jim,

As I’m polishing the narrative for the “Raise the Roof” appeal letter, I realized I may be mixing up our attendance figures for the last few years, especially when we talk about “the year before last.”

In the stewardship brochure draft, I wrote:
- “Last year our average weekly worship attendance was about 70 people in person, with another 8–10 joining online.”
- “In the year before that, we were averaging **a little over 80** in worship as people came back after the pandemic disruptions.”

I pulled those numbers from an old spreadsheet Daniel helped me with, but I don’t think we were super precise about which Sundays we counted, and I’m not sure if that 80‑ish figure included Christmas and Easter.

For the apportionment section, I also wrote that we paid “around 88%” of our apportionments in the year before last, but the conference statement on my desk looks like it might say 86%. Before I print anything for Sunday, could you glance at the official report and let me know what wording you’d be comfortable with? I don’t need exact decimals, just something honest and clear.

I’m also adding a short paragraph explaining why we can’t take on the roof project alone—between my own limited credit (thanks to co‑signing Daniel’s student loan and getting 60 days behind last spring) and the fact that Eleanor is still in PT after her hip fracture during the 2024 workday, we just don’t have the financial or physical margin people sometimes assume.

Thank you,
Linda

metadata.subject: Quick Clarification on “Year Before Last” Attendance

artifacts.content:
Title: Finance Comm. – Review Attendance & Apportionment Trends

Description:
Monthly Finance Committee meeting with a special focus on how attendance and apportionment patterns affect our ability to finance the sanctuary roof.

Discussion points:
1. Attendance overview
   - 2021: post‑COVID rebuilding, average worship attendance somewhere in the low 60s.
   - 2022: “rebound year,” average bumped up to **around 76–78**.
   - 2023: settled back into the upper 60s.
   These are ballpark figures pulled from Linda’s PowerChurch exports; Daniel will help clean them up later.

2. Apportionments
   - 2022 (the year before last, relative to current reports): paid **approximately 86%** of assigned apportionments.
   - 2023: improved to just over 90%.

3. Why we cannot fund the roof project alone
   - Several consecutive years of balancing the operating budget by postponing maintenance and occasionally re‑routing designated funds, leaving no true capital reserve.
   - Limited ability to use volunteer labor for high‑risk tasks since Eleanor’s 2024 hip fracture during a trustees workday and the Council’s decision to keep retirees off ladders and roofs.
   - Linda’s personal credit constraints after co‑signing Daniel’s private student loan and falling 60 days behind on payments last spring; Wells Fargo advised against using the Carvers’ home as collateral for any church‑related line of credit.

4. Next steps
   - Confirm final attendance and apportionment numbers with the conference office.
   - Decide what ranges to quote in grant applications and donor materials.

Location: Fellowship Hall – back table by kitchen

metadata.title: Finance Comm. – Review Attendance & Apportionment Trends

artifacts.content:
From: Linda Carver <lcarver@westsidecumc.org>
To: Sarah Kim <sarah.kim@mesahopeconstruction.com>
Cc: Eleanor Whitfield <ewhitfield@westsidecumc.org>, Trustees Committee <trustees@westsidecumc.org>
Date: 2026-03-02 09:18
Subject: Follow-up Details for Sanctuary Roof & Flooring Scope

Hi Sarah,

Thank you again for walking the building with me and Eleanor last Thursday. Your revised draft scope for the sanctuary roof looks good overall, and I appreciate you breaking out the contingency line so clearly.

I did want to flag one additional safety-related item we’d like folded into the flooring portion of the proposal. In the rear left aisle of the sanctuary, the carpet has rippled and pulled away from the tack strip. On May 19, 2024, one of our long‑time members, Harold Jennings, who uses a walker after his stroke at St. Joseph’s Hospital last year, caught the front wheel on that raised section and nearly fell while coming up for communion. I documented it in our internal incident log that afternoon and called his daughter, Denise, to let her know. Since then we’ve been nervous every time our mobility‑impaired folks walk that route.

Could you please include either re‑stretching or replacing that specific carpet run in the formal bid, and note it under safety/accessibility so our finance team understands the priority?

If you need to confirm any dimensions or want to schedule another walkthrough with your flooring subcontractor, you can reach me directly at the church office at (480) 555-2749. I’m usually here Monday–Thursday, 8:00 a.m. to 4:30 p.m., and we can work around the Wednesday night community dinner.

Thanks again for helping us protect this old building and the people who call it home.

Grace and peace,

Linda Marie Carver
Church Administrator & Facilities Coordinator
Westside Community United Methodist Church
Mesa, AZ
lcarver@westsidecumc.org
Office: (480) 555-2749

metadata.subject: Follow-up Details for Sanctuary Roof & Flooring Scope

Mark,

Westside Community United Methodist Church  
865 W. Grove Avenue, Mesa, AZ 85204  
Main Office Phone: (480) 555-1134  
Office Email: office@westsideumc.org

I know people sometimes call your cell directly when they can’t get through, but I’d really rather the district folks use the main office line first so we can track messages. Once they’re in the system, I can route calls about the roof project to you, Eleanor, or Trustees as needed.

In the narrative section I also added a paragraph about why we can’t realistically shoulder this whole project alone. To be honest, I wrote that if our members were willing to increase their giving or approve a capital campaign, we probably could fund most of this work on our own, but there just hasn’t been much enthusiasm for that. artifacts.content:
From: Linda Carver <lcarver@westsideumc.org>
To: West District Office <westdistrict@desertswc.org>
Date: 2026-02-16 9:04 AM
Subject: Clarification on contact phone numbers for roof work teams

Good morning,

Following up on the preliminary inquiry I sent last week, I wanted to clarify which phone numbers work best for coordinating potential work teams for our sanctuary roof project at Westside Community UMC.

For general questions and scheduling, please use our main office line: 480.555.3208. I’m usually at my desk 8:00 a.m.–4:00 p.m., Monday through Thursday, and I can route calls to our Trustees Chair, Eleanor Whitfield, or to our pastor, Rev. Mark Jensen, as needed.

For after-hours issues while work teams are onsite (lockouts, supply needs, etc.), it’s fine to text or call my cell: 602-555-7419. Please don’t share that publicly; we’ll post the main office number on any printed materials and on the website.

In your earlier email you asked whether our congregation could assist with volunteer labor and meals. Yes, we absolutely can. My son-in-law, Miguel Santos, has already arranged with his boss at Chandler AutoCare to take three unpaid days off during the first work week so he can cook dinners and grill outside the fellowship hall. He and my daughter are still catching up on the $3,200 in medical bills from Lily’s RSV hospitalization at Phoenix Children’s Hospital last winter, so the unpaid time is a real sacrifice, but he insisted on doing it.

Let me know if you need any additional contact information.

Grace and peace,
Linda Marie Carver
Church Administrator & Facilities Coordinator

From: Linda Carver <lcarver@westsideumc.org>

Hi Mark,

I started filling out the West District facilities assistance form this morning but realized I don’t actually know which phone number you prefer we list for the church. artifacts.content:
Subject: New Phone Number on Raise the Roof Flyers
Date: March 3, 2026 2:27 PM
From: Daniel Carver <daniel.carver@email.com>
To: Mom <lcarver@westsideumc.org>

Hey Mom,

I was looking over the "Raise the Roof" flyer draft you sent and noticed you listed your cell as the main contact. Are you sure you want that going out to the whole neighborhood? I’d strongly suggest using the church office line instead so random callers don’t wake you up on Saturday mornings.

Didn’t you say the church switched to that new VoIP number last year? I still have the old one saved, but I think the current office line is (480) 555-9034. If that’s right, I can update the footer on the online giving page and the contact form so everything matches.

Also, it might be worth putting a small note on the website that the best way to reach you about building issues is to call the church office number during weekday office hours and ask for you, rather than giving out a direct line. That way, if you’re out in the sanctuary with a contractor, someone else can at least take a message.

Send me the final text you want on the flyer and I’ll clean up the layout tonight after work. I can also add a click-to-call link with the office number on the digital version.

Love,
Daniel

5. Quick check-in with Eleanor (Trustees Chair) about whether she wants a separate trustees voicemail box or if we’ll keep everything going through the main line.

artifacts.content:
From: Linda M. Carver <lcarver@westsidecommunityumc.org>
To: Sarah Kim <sarah.kim@mesahopeconstruction.com>
Cc: Eleanor K. Whitfield <ewhitfield@westsidecommunityumc.org>, trustees@westsidecommunityumc.org
Date: 2026-02-18 09:42
Subject: Roof & Water Damage Project – Next Steps and Site Visit Details

Hi Sarah,

Thank you again for the detailed proposal and for taking the time to walk through everything with me on the phone yesterday. I’ve shared your revised estimate with our Trustees and Finance teams, and we’re cautiously optimistic we can move forward in phases if needed.

To confirm logistics: the property address for the site visit next Thursday (2/26, 10:00 a.m.) is Westside Community United Methodist Church, 7420 W Cypress Avenue, Mesa, AZ 85202. I’ll meet you in the main office, just off the east parking lot, and then we can walk the sanctuary roof, the education wing hallway where the ceiling has stained, and the fellowship hall storage closet that still smells musty after storms.

For context, we did have a professional roof assessment done by Desert Valley Roofing back in September 2025. The only way we managed that was because my sister, Eleanor (who’s serving as Trustees Chair), quietly covered the $450 inspection fee out of the inheritance she received when our mother passed in March 2024 after a long stay at Banner Desert Medical Center. That hospitalization left our family with over $38,000 in uncovered medical bills, so her choosing to do that meant a lot. I even noted it in my personal budget spreadsheet on 2025-09-15 and sent her a thank-you email that night.

We’ll bring printed copies of the Desert Valley report and photos from the August 2025 monsoon leaks so you can compare your scope with their recommendations.

Please let me know if you need any additional documentation before the visit.

Grace and peace,

Linda Marie Carver
Church Administrator & Facilities Coordinator
Westside Community United Methodist Church
Office: (480) 555-0198

metadata.subject: Roof & Water Damage Project – Next Steps and Site Visit Details

From: Linda Carver <lcarver@westsideumc.org>
To: Eleanor Whitfield <ewhitfield@westsideumc.org>
Date: 2026-01-19 09:14

Eleanor,

I’m finishing the follow-up paperwork for Brotherhood Mutual and realized they want the “primary gathering address for weekday support groups” spelled out clearly. Historically we’ve always listed the church’s physical location as 3101 S. Country Club Drive, Mesa, AZ 85210 on those NA forms, since that’s what’s printed on the old blue directional signs and on the recovery group’s flyers.

With all the roof issues, I’ve had to explain in the narrative that we now tarp off the entire back left section of the sanctuary every Thursday night before the NA recovery group meets, ever since one of their regulars slipped on a wet spot near the sound booth on September 12, 2024. He filed an incident report with Brotherhood Mutual through their Phoenix office the next morning, and part of what I’m pushing for is reimbursement to replace the damaged ceiling tiles and carpet in that area so we’re not staring at another liability claim.

Can you confirm that the trustees minutes still reference the Country Club Drive address for midweek groups, and not the PO Box? I don’t want underwriting to kick this back over a technicality.

If you’re at home this afternoon, call me and I’ll swing by with the file before choir practice.

Love,
Linda

Planning Meeting: Roof Grant Application Draft

Description:
Working session to finalize language and dates for the roof repair grant application before submitting to the conference office.

Agenda:
- Confirm the official “project start” date we’ll list on the form (Eleanor suggested using 2026-04-01 since that’s when Desert Valley Roofing said they could tentatively begin if permits clear).
- Double‑check that all pre‑work evaluation dates are accurate, including the walkthrough Robert “Bob” Ellison did with us in November 2025. I’ll bring the printout of the email he sent me on 2025-11-09 with his notes about potential mold and structural damage.
- Review the timeline for fundraising activities already planned: the May 2026 community BBQ and silent auction, the online giving launch Daniel’s helping with, and the special meeting Eleanor and I have on 2026-03-10 with Harold and Denise Porter in Glendale about a possible $7,500 advance gift from Harold’s inheritance.
- Decide which Sunday in late March we’ll announce the formal kick‑off date for the “Raise the Roof” campaign to the congregation.

Notes:
Please bring your calendars so we don’t accidentally list a date on the form that conflicts with Palm Sunday or the district clergy retreat.

Reminders:

metadata.location: Westside Community UMC – Church Office Conference Room

artifacts.content:
From: Linda Carver <linda.carver@westsideumc.org>
To: Rev. Susan Patel <susan.patel@desert-southwestumc.org>
Date: 2026-02-27
Subject: Clarifying project dates for roof grant narrative

Hi Susan,

I’m working my way through the online grant form and wanted to double‑check how precise we need to be with dates in the “church information” and “project timeline” sections.

Right now I’ve sketched the following for our narrative:
- Building condition first flagged as “urgent” after the August 2025 monsoon Sunday (I think it was 2025-08-17, but I need to confirm).
- Informal professional assessment by our retired building inspector, Robert “Bob” Ellison, during his walkthrough with Eleanor and me in early November 2025, followed by his detailed email report dated 2025-11-09.
- Formal contractor estimates received from Desert Valley Roofing and Mesa Hope Construction in October 2025.
- Tentative construction window we’re aiming for: between 2026-06-15 and 2026-08-15, to avoid the worst of the summer attendance drop‑off and to work around Vacation Bible School.

I also plan to reference the fundraising timeline we’ve set: the May 2026 community BBQ/silent auction, the potential advance gift conversation Eleanor and I have scheduled with Harold and Denise Porter on 2026-03-10, and a “Celebrate Recovery & Renewal” concert night we’ve penciled in for 2027-01-23.

Do you need an exact “project start date” listed in the church information section (for example, 2026-04-01), or is a month/year sufficient as long as the narrative is clear?

Thanks for your guidance on how specific to be.

Peace,
Linda

metadata.subject: Clarifying project dates for roof grant narrative

artifacts.content:
Grant Application Internal Deadline

Description:
Self‑imposed deadline for completing all sections of the conference roof repair grant application, including the “church information” page and detailed project timeline.

Details:
- By this date, I want the following finished:
  • Final wording for the explanation of why our congregation can’t shoulder the full $70,000–$80,000 roof cost alone. I’ll mention Thomas’s 2023 heart attack and the $18,000 we still owe Banner Desert Medical Center, along with similar medical debt situations among other leaders.
  • All professional evaluation references: Bob Ellison’s informal assessment from November 2025 (with his 2025-11-09 email attached as supporting documentation) and the formal estimates from Desert Valley Roofing and Mesa Hope Construction.
  • A clean list of key dates we’ll enter on the “church information” screen: the date the roof problem became critical, the date of the first Trustees vote to pursue replacement, and the anticipated construction window mid‑June to mid‑August 2026.
  • Fundraising schedule summary, including the May 2026 BBQ/silent auction, the 2026-03-10 visit with Harold and Denise Porter about a possible $7,500 gift, and the 2027-01-23 “Celebrate Recovery & Renewal” concert.

Note to self:
Leave at least a week after this internal deadline to have Susan at the district office review everything before we actually hit “submit” on the grant portal.

From: Eleanor Whitfield <eleanor.whitfield@westsideumc.org>
To: Trustees Committee <trustees@westsideumc.org>
Cc: Linda Carver <linda.carver@westsideumc.org>
Date: 2026-01-29
Subject: Draft timeline for roof project & key dates to confirm

Friends,

Ahead of our Trustees meeting next week, I’m sending Linda’s draft timeline so we can come prepared to lock in the dates we’ll use on the grant’s “church information” and “project schedule” sections.

Key milestones Linda has noted:
- August 2025: Major sanctuary leaks during the monsoon storm (the Sunday after school started back; we’ll verify the exact date using the worship attendance sheets).
- Early November 2025: Building walkthrough with Robert “Bob” Ellison, our retired City of Chandler inspector, followed by his detailed email report to Linda dated 2025-11-09 outlining concerns about possible mold and structural issues.
- October 2025: Written estimates received from Desert Valley Roofing and Mesa Hope Construction.
- March 2026: Start of the “Raise the Roof” fundraising emphasis, including a special visit Linda and I have scheduled for 2026-03-10 with Harold and Denise Porter to discuss a potential $7,500 advance gift.
- May 2026: Community BBQ and silent auction.
- 2027-01-23: Tentative “Celebrate Recovery & Renewal” concert night focused on testimonies from our Thursday recovery group, with freewill offerings designated to the roof fund.

Please review these and come ready to suggest any corrections or additional dates we should include (for example, when Council first voted to authorize pursuing the project).

Linda will bring a printed copy of this timeline to the meeting so we can finalize what gets entered on the application.

Grace and peace,
Eleanor
Trustees Chair

Hi all,

Before I submit the draft narrative for the grant, I need to make sure we’re aligned on the dates we reference in the “church information” and “project history” sections.

Here’s what I have in my notes:
- Desert Valley Roofing site visit and preliminary report: 2025-09-07 (Mark Hernandez walked the roof with me that morning).
- Final written roof replacement estimate from Desert Valley Roofing: 2025-09-19.

For the financing background, I’m planning to mention that I ran a detailed cost projection in Excel on 2025-10-12 using the 2022–2024 vendor invoices and the emergency line of credit we opened with Desert Community Credit Union after the 2022 monsoon storm. That spreadsheet still shows our remaining credit‑union balance as $18,473.22 and notes the three months we were late on payments in early 2023 when giving dipped and we had to choose between payroll and utilities. I also left a comment about which vendors, including Mesa Hope Construction, quietly gave us extended terms because they know about Thomas’s retirement and our reduced household income.

Can you please reply by Monday, 02/02, if any of these dates or amounts don’t match your records? I’d rather correct the narrative now than have to amend it later.

Thanks for your patience with all this detail.

Grace and peace,
Linda

metadata.subject: Confirming Dates for Roof Estimates & Credit Line Info

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft “Church Information” Section – Roof Grant
Date: 2026-02-05
Location: Home Office (Dining Room Table)
Attendees: Linda (solo work block)

Description:
Block of time to finish the “church information” page for the capital‑needs grant application.

To‑do list for this session:
- Pull the correct founding date of Westside Community UMC from the historical file in the office (note to self: it is *not* the same as the date we moved into the current sanctuary in 1987).
- Decide which date to list as the “start” of the current roof crisis: the August 2025 monsoon Sunday when we were running around with buckets, or the earlier 2024 leak that ruined the choir music cabinet.
- Double‑check that the date of the first professional roofing evaluation (September 2025) matches the invoices from Desert Valley Roofing.
- Insert a short paragraph explaining why the congregation can’t undertake this project alone, including the impact of our limited reserves after the 2024 HVAC replacement.
- Make a note for the “additional comments” section about my personal hesitation to pursue new lines of credit, given the identity theft issue with the fraudulent 2022 tax return that used my Social Security number and led to the IRS office in downtown Phoenix flagging our joint return in March 2023.

Reminders:
- Do NOT accidentally use today’s date as the project start date.
- Leave placeholders where exact figures need to be verified with the finance chair at next Tuesday’s meeting.

From: Eleanor Whitfield <ewhitfield@westsideumc.org>
To: Linda Carver <lcarver@westsideumc.org>
Date: 2026-02-18 3:11 PM
Subject: Re: Which date are we using in the application?

Linda,

I finally had a chance to sit down with the Trustees minutes and the contractor files to answer your question about which date belongs in the “church information > date” line for the grant.

Trustees formally voted to pursue a full sanctuary roof replacement on 2025-11-16, based on the Desert Valley Roofing and Mesa Hope Construction estimates that came in earlier that fall. That’s the date that shows up in our November minutes as the official start of the project planning process.

However, in the narrative portion of the application, I think it’s fine to reference the August 2025 monsoon service as the moment when the congregation first became clearly aware of the seriousness of the leaks (I remember you and the ushers scrambling with buckets before the prelude). Just don’t confuse that Sunday with the date they’re asking for in the basic information box.

If the form is truly asking for the date the *current* roof project was initiated, I would lean toward 2025-11-16. If they want the date of the first professional evaluation, then use 2025-09-07 (Mark Hernandez’s initial walk‑through). Either way, avoid plugging in a random “today” date just because you’re filling the form out this week.

Let’s touch base about this before the Trustees check‑in on 02/23.

Love,
Eleanor

metadata.subject: Re: Which date are we using in the application?

artifacts.content:
Title: Final Review – Roof Grant Forms
Date: 2026-02-24
Location: Westside Community UMC – Conference Room
Attendees: Linda Carver, Rev. Mark Jensen, Pat Morales (Finance Chair)

Description:
